title: PsiQuantum has a plan to make a massive quantum computer out of light
gist: PsiQuantum, a photonic-qubit quantum computing startup, has raised $1 billion, broken ground on manufacturing-scale sites in Chicago and Australia (targeting 'operational' cooling infrastructure by end of 2027, not a full working computer), and reached the third stage of a DARPA benchmarking program alongside only Microsoft — but its approach remains a black box to outside experts and its published results so far show only modest speedups over classical computers.

themes.theme: Funding, government validation, and infrastructure scale

insights.claim: PsiQuantum raised $1 billion in funding in 2025 and broke ground on a manufacturing site in Chicago built in partnership with local governments, plus a second site in Moreton Bay, Australia, which the company says will be 'operational' (hardware-ready, cooling systems installed) by the end of 2027.
insights.speaker: James O'Donnell
insights.so_what: Signals massive capital commitment and government backing (state/local partnerships) behind a pre-revenue, pre-working-product quantum computing bet — a key data point for gauging capital intensity and burn in the quantum computing race.
insights.quote: Last year, PsiQuantum raised $1 billion in funding and broke ground in Chicago on a site it’s building in partnership with local governments. It also has a second site in the works in Australia, which it promises will be operational—meaning hardware-ready—in 2027.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "In a field full of such claims"

insights.claim: PsiQuantum is one of only two companies, alongside Microsoft, to reach the third stage of a US government (DARPA) benchmarking initiative evaluating which quantum computing companies are likely to succeed; DARPA's program lead said in a 2026 statement that 'it now seems likely that someone will build a utility-scale quantum computer by 2033.'
insights.speaker: Micah Stoutimore (cited by James O'Donnell)
insights.so_what: DARPA acts as a quasi-independent validator in a field where outside verification is otherwise very hard; being one of two firms at stage three is a meaningful competitive signal for investors comparing quantum computing bets.
insights.quote: it now seems likely that someone will build a utility-scale quantum computer by 2033
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "The outsider with perhaps the best guess"

insights.claim: The cooling infrastructure required to run PsiQuantum's photonic quantum computers — liquid-helium systems that bring detector chips to about 2 K (-456°F) — represents one of the largest capital expenditures for the company and consumes a significant portion of its $1 billion funding round.
insights.speaker: James O'Donnell
insights.so_what: Cooling infrastructure cost is a major, non-obvious cost center in quantum computing economics that investors should model separately from chip R&D spend.
insights.quote: These helium systems represent some of the biggest capital expenditures for any quantum company and will consume a significant chunk of PsiQuantum’s $1 billion funding round.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "The siphoning setup was a temporary solution"

insights.claim: PsiQuantum has been awarded $100 million in CHIPS Act funding, which it said in May it will spend partly on its single-photon detector systems.
insights.speaker: James O'Donnell
insights.so_what: Direct US federal industrial-policy funding for a private quantum computing startup underscores the extent to which the US government is subsidizing this race, alongside GlobalFoundries which also received CHIPS Act funds.
insights.quote: PsiQuantum said in May that it will spend some of the $100 million award in CHIPS Act funding it’s slated to get on these detectors
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "Right now the cabinets operate at 2 K"

themes.theme: Photonic qubit technical bet vs. rivals

insights.claim: PsiQuantum is building its quantum computer using photons (particles of light) as qubits, differentiating it from Google and IBM (superconducting circuits) and Intel (electron-based qubits) — a bet enabled by a 2001 Los Alamos/University of Queensland paper showing that photon interactions needed for computation can be faked using networks of beam splitters and detectors.
insights.speaker: James O'Donnell
insights.so_what: Frames the core technology divergence among quantum computing leaders — investors comparing Google, IBM, Intel, and PsiQuantum should treat qubit modality as the primary technical risk/differentiation axis, not just funding or roadmap dates.
insights.quote: Google and IBM are betting on superconducting qubits... Intel is using electrons. PsiQuantum is using photons, the particles that make up light.

insights.claim: Unlike superconducting quantum computing approaches that require cooling the entire machine to near absolute zero, PsiQuantum's photonic architecture requires deep cooling only for the single-photon detectors at the end of the computation chain, which the company argues could make its refrigeration costs structurally lower.
insights.speaker: James O'Donnell
insights.so_what: A potential structural cost advantage over superconducting-qubit competitors (Google, IBM) if it holds at scale — a key variable for comparing long-run unit economics across quantum computing approaches.
insights.quote: Most quantum approaches require the whole machine to be cooled to superconducting temperatures, so that much of the expense in running it will actually be spent on refrigeration. But photonic computers require only one piece to be this cold—the detectors that measure single photons at the end of the computation.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "Right now the cabinets operate at 2 K"

insights.claim: PsiQuantum manufactures its own barium titanate crystal in-house — a material needed to route individual photons with minimal electrical input — because it was not available at industrial scale when the company started; the crystal discs are then shipped to chipmaker GlobalFoundries in Malta, New York, for chip fabrication, leveraging the existing silicon photonic chip supply chain already used in data centers.
insights.speaker: James O'Donnell
insights.so_what: PsiQuantum's bet is that reusing an existing semiconductor supply chain (vs. building fully bespoke fabs like some rivals) will make it more efficient at scale — a distinct capital-efficiency thesis worth tracking against actual yield and cost data as production ramps.
insights.quote: PsiQuantum’s bet is that this entire supply chain, byzantine as it might sound, will make the company more efficient than its competitors. That’s because, if you squint, it looks like a souped-up and high-precision version of the existing supply chain for silicon photonic chips.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "PsiQuantum’s bet is that this entire supply chain"

insights.claim: As of the article's reporting, PsiQuantum's Milpitas, California test facility has connected three cabinets of 250 chips each (750 chips total), and the company's next milestone is scaling that up toward roughly 100 cabinets connected together once its Australian cooling system arrives in late 2027, to test whether its error-correction techniques hold up at scale.
insights.speaker: James O'Donnell
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete, verifiable current scale (750 chips) against the ~100-cabinet target needed for a commercially useful machine — a benchmark investors can track over coming quarters as a leading indicator of execution progress.
insights.quote: its Milpitas site has connected three cabinets together, with 250 chips in each, but the next step is to scale the systems up and see whether the company’s techniques for correcting errors can keep up.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "But it’s not a given that one working chip"

themes.theme: Timeline ambiguity across the quantum industry

insights.claim: News reports have said 2027 is the year PsiQuantum's Australian site will have a full-scale quantum computer online, but the company insists this is a misreading and that it only commits to the facility being 'operational' — meaning cooling systems installed and ready for hardware — by then, with no promise on the resulting computer's size.
insights.speaker: James O'Donnell
insights.so_what: A material expectations gap between public narrative and PsiQuantum's actual committed milestone — investors and journalists tracking 'PsiQuantum 2027' headlines should discount for this ambiguity when assessing delivery risk.
insights.quote: the company insists the deadline has been misread, and that it only intends for its facility to be “operational” by the end of next year. That means cooling systems in place and ready for hardware to be installed, but no promises about what size computer will be ready.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "News articles have said that 2027"

insights.claim: IBM originally targeted 2028 for a large-scale, error-corrected quantum system under its 2020 development roadmap, but that deadline now appears to have slipped to 2030 — illustrating a pattern of timeline slippage across the quantum computing industry, not just at PsiQuantum.
insights.speaker: James O'Donnell
insights.so_what: Cross-company evidence that quantum computing delivery dates are systematically optimistic; investors should apply a consistent slippage discount across the sector, not just to PsiQuantum specifically.
insights.quote: It initially targeted 2028 for a large-scale, error-corrected system, a deadline that now appears to have been pushed out to 2030.

insights.claim: PsiQuantum VP of quantum applications Philipp Ernst says the company aims to reduce the time to estimate how cytochrome P450 enzymes (which break down drugs in the body) will interact with a specific drug molecule from over 10 years with current methods down to four minutes using its quantum computer.
insights.speaker: Philipp Ernst (cited by James O'Donnell)
insights.so_what: This is PsiQuantum's flagship pharma use case and a benchmark claim (10 years to 4 minutes) that, if realized, would be transformative for drug discovery economics — but it depends entirely on hardware that does not yet exist, so treat as aspirational, not near-term.
insights.quote: we aim to get it down to four minutes
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "Consider the company’s hopes for predicting"

insights.claim: PsiQuantum has announced customer partnerships with Lockheed Martin (materials design), Mercedes (battery design), and Airbus (fluid dynamics), even though none of these companies has actual quantum hardware to run algorithms on yet — PsiQuantum argues this is analogous to developers writing games for an unreleased game console.
insights.speaker: James O'Donnell
insights.so_what: Shows PsiQuantum is pre-selling algorithm-development relationships with major industrials well ahead of hardware delivery — a customer-pipeline signal, though monetization timing remains tied to the same uncertain hardware timeline.
insights.quote: There’s a PlayStation 6 probably coming up from Sony next year or the year after, and people are programming those games right now... This is, in principle, very similar.

insights.claim: A December 2025 PsiQuantum paper with Airbus tested a new algorithm modeling airplane-wing turbulence and found only a moderate speed increase over classical computers; outside expert Andrew Childs said such speedups are unlikely to have significant practical impact until very large-scale quantum computers exist, a view PsiQuantum's own VP agreed with.
insights.speaker: Andrew Childs (cited by James O'Donnell)
insights.so_what: An independent expert's tempered assessment of PsiQuantum's own published results is a useful counterweight to the company's marketing claims — current published quantum advantage results remain modest, not breakthrough.
insights.quote: It’s probably unlikely that speedups like this will have a significant practical impact until we have very large-scale quantum computers.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "The company also published a paper in December"

insights.claim: PsiQuantum is researching how long its systems would take to run Shor's algorithm (1994), which could break encryption widely used for credit card numbers and military intelligence; the fact that no one yet has a computer capable of running it is described as 'the thing keeping the world together, for now,' and security researchers are already developing quantum-resistant encryption in anticipation.
insights.speaker: James O'Donnell
insights.so_what: Frames quantum computing progress as a direct countdown clock on current encryption standards — relevant to cybersecurity vendors building post-quantum cryptography and to any sector (finance, defense) exposed to encryption-breaking risk.
insights.quote: The thing keeping the world together, for now, is that nobody has a computer to run the algorithm on (and security experts are already launching new encryption methods that could withstand attacks from a quantum computer).

insights.claim: Theoretical computer scientist Scott Aaronson (UT Austin) says it is very hard for outsiders to evaluate PsiQuantum's progress, unlike Google and Quantinuum, which regularly publish incremental engineering results demonstrating their progress toward large machines.
insights.speaker: Scott Aaronson (cited by James O'Donnell)
insights.so_what: A credible independent voice flags PsiQuantum as comparatively opaque versus peers who publish verifiable incremental milestones — investors should weight PsiQuantum's self-reported progress claims accordingly and seek third-party verification where possible.
insights.quote: It is very hard for an outsider to evaluate
insights.locator: paragraph beginning "But to the rest of the industry"
